analogy: Think of "Colonialism and the Countryside" through the chapter's own opening images: 227 In this chapter you will see what colonial rule meant to those who lived in the countryside. You will meet the zamindars of Bengal, travel to the Rajmahal hills where the Paharias and the Santhals lived, and then move west to the Deccan. You... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: THEME, NINE, THEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART III 228, 1.1 An auction in Burdwan. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Colonialism and the Countryside is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 227 In this chapter you will see what colonial rule meant to those who lived in the countryside.

The text explains that you will meet the zamindars of Bengal, travel to the Rajmahal hills where the Paharias and the Santhals lived, and then move west to the Deccan.

The text explains that you will look at the way the English East India Company (E.I.C.) established its raj in the countryside, implemented its revenue policies, what these policies meant to different sections of people, and how they changed everyday lives.

The text explains that laws introduced by the state have consequences for people: they determine to an extent who grows richer and who poorer, who acquires new land and who loses the land they have lived on, where peasants go when they need money.

The text explains that as you will see, however, people were not only subject to the working of laws, they also resisted the law by acting according to what they believed to be just.

The text explains that in doing so people defined the way in which laws operated, thereby modifying their consequences.

The text explains that you will also come to know about the sources that tell us about these histories, and the problems historians face in interpreting them.

The text explains that you will read about revenue records and surveys, journals and accounts left by surveyors and travellers, and reports produced by enquiry commissions.

The text explains that 9.1 Cotton being carried from the village to the mandi, Illustrated London News, 20 April 1861 Colonialism and the Countryside Explor Explor Explor Explor Exploring Of ing Of ing Of ing Of ing Off f f f ficial Ar icial Ar icial Ar icial Ar icial Arc c c c chiv hiv hiv hiv hives es es es es THEME NINE

The text explains that bengal and the Zamindars As you know, colonial rule was first established in Bengal.

The text explains that it is here that the earliest attempts were made to reorder rural society and establish a new regime of land rights and a new revenue system.

The text explains that let us see what happened in Bengal in the early years of Company (E.I.C.) rule.

The text explains that 1.1 An auction in Burdwan In 1797 there was an auction in Burdwan (present- day Bardhaman).

The text explains that a number of mahals (estates) held by the Raja of Burdwan were being sold.

The text explains that the Permanent Settlement had come into operation in 1793.

The text explains that the East India Company had fixed the revenue that each zamindar had to pay.

The text explains that by the 1770s, the rural economy in Bengal was in crisis, with recurrent famines and declining agricultural output.

The text explains that officials felt that agriculture, trade and the revenue resources of the state could all be developed by encouraging investment in agriculture.

The text explains that this could be done by securing rights of property and permanently fixing the rates of Raja (literally king) was a term that was often used to designate powerful zamindars.

The text explains that the process, officials hoped, would lead to the emergence of a class of yeomen farmers and rich landowners who would have the capital and enterprise to improve agriculture.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART III 230 demand high, arguing that the burden on zamindars would gradually decline as agricultural production expanded and prices rose.

The text explains that zamindars could prosecute defaulters, but the judicial process was long drawn.

The text explains that ryot is the way the term raiyat, used to designate peasants (Chapter 8), was spelt in British records.

The text explains that ryots in Bengal did not always cultivate the land directly, but leased it out to under-ryots.

The text explains that unlike zamindars who often lived in urban areas, jotedars were located in the villages and exercised direct control over a considerable section of poor villagers.

The text explains that jotedars and moneylenders in rural Bengal lived in houses like the one you see on the right.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART III 232 The jotedars of Dinajpur Buchanan described the ways in which the jotedars of Dinajpur in North Bengal resisted being disciplined by the zamindar and undermined his power: Landlords do not like this class of men, but it is evident that they are absolutely necessary, unless the landlords themselves would advance money to their necessitous tenantry … The jotedars who cultivate large portions of lands are very refractory, and know that the zamindars have no power over them.

The text explains that faced with an exorbitantly high revenue demand and possible auction of their estates, they devised ways of surviving the pressures.

The text explains that this process was repeated endlessly, exhausting the state, and the other bidders at the auction.

The text explains that the Source 1 Ü Describe the ways in which the jotedars resisted the authority of the zamindars. Ü Read the text accompanying Fig.9.5 carefully and insert the following terms in appropriate places along the arrows: rent, revenue, interest, loan, produce Fig.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART III 234 groups in Britain who were opposed to the monopoly that the East India Company had over trade with India and China.

The text explains that compare the account of the zamindars you have just read with that in Chapter 8. Ü From the tone in which evidence is recorded, what do you think is the attitude of the report to the facts narrated?

The text explains that researchers have carefully examined the archives of various Bengal zamindars and the local records of the districts to write about the history of colonial rule in rural Bengal.

The text explains that you will see the changes that came about when the frontiers of the peasant economy expanded outwards, swallowing up pastures and forests in the Rajmahal hills.

The text explains that it raises questions in our mind, but does not always help us answer them.

The text explains that it tells us about a moment in time, but not about the longer history of people and places.

The text explains that on the request of the Government of Bengal, he undertook detailed surveys of the areas under the jurisdiction of the British East India Company.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART III 238 of their identity as well as survival; and they resisted the intrusion of outsiders.

The text explains that to the British, extension of settled agriculture was necessary to enlarge the sources of land revenue, produce crops for export, and establish the basis of a settled, ordered society.

The text explains that 9.9 A view of Jangal territory, painted by William Hodges Here you can see the forested low hills and the rocky upper ranges, nowhere actually above 2,000 feet.

The text explains that being in the pay of the colonial government, they came to be perceived as subordinate employees or stipendiary chiefs.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART III 240 2.2 The Santhals: Pioneer settlers At the end of 1810, Buchanan crossed Ganjuria Pahar, which was part of the Rajmahal ranges, passed through the rocky country beyond, and reached a village.

The text explains that i think its beauty and riches might be made equal to almost any in the universe.” The soil here was rocky but “uncommonly fine”, and nowhere had Buchanan seen finer tobacco and mustard.

The text explains that santhal myths and songs of the nineteenth century refer very frequently to a long history of travel: they represent the Santhal past as one of continuous mobility, a tireless search for a place to settle.

The text explains that restricted from moving down to the lower hills and valleys, they were confined to the dry interior and to the more barren and rocky upper hills.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART III 242 The Santhals, however, soon found that the land they had brought under cultivation was slipping away from their hands.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART III 244 2.3 The accounts of Buchanan We have been drawing on Buchanan’s account, but while reading his reports we should not forget that he was an employee of the British East India Company.

The text explains that as the Company consolidated its power and expanded its commerce, it looked for natural resources it could control and exploit.

The text explains that it surveyed landscapes and revenue sources, organised voyages of discovery, and sent its geologists and geographers, its botanists and medical men to collect information.

The text explains that highlight the ways in which historians can use such texts.

The text explains that in the region where you live, talk to the older people within a rural community and visit the fields they now cultivate.

The text explains that find out what they produce, how they earn their livelihoods, what their parents did, what their sons and daughters do now, and how their lives have changed over the last 75 years.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Themes in Indian History III, Chapter 1: Colonialism and the Countryside

prelims_traps: Do not treat THEME and NINE as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Colonialism and the Countryside; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
